On the long-form / short-form rhythm of a brand system.
The brand system that survives is the one that knows when to whisper. A note on visual cadence, drawn from three years of identity work.
Art Director and co-founder at Studio Nord. Twelve years on the typesetting end of a deadline.
abstract typographic composition, black and white

import Marginalia from "../../components/Marginalia.astro";

A brand system is a piece of music. There is a tempo, a chorus, a verse, and a long bridge where very little happens at all. The mistake we see most often — in pitch decks, in design audits, in the marketing pages of design tools — is to treat every surface as a chorus.

The short-form moments — the logotype on a coffee bag, the favicon, the launch tweet — earn their loudness by being rare. The long-form moments — the brand book, the editorial pages, the email footer — earn their quietness by being everywhere.

Lay out twenty surfaces of the brand in front of you. If three of them are louder than the other seventeen, the system is breathing. If twelve are loud, the system is shouting. Edit until it whispers, then add one shout back.

"A brand system that survives a quarter is one whose chorus you remember. A brand system that survives a decade is one whose verse you can still hum."

The shape of a verse

Most of our work on a brand system is on the verse — the part nobody photographs. A grid that holds a 14-point body in a 380-pixel column. A footer that doesn't beg for attention. A loading state that isn't trying to be charming. A 404 page that simply says, in good type, that the page isn't there.

You can audit the verse with a single sentence: what does a customer see for the longest, on the dullest day? Whatever the answer is — usually a transactional email or a checkout page — that is the surface that needs the most care. 

{`/* The rhythm in code */
:root {
  --tempo-loud: clamp(3.5rem, 8vw, 9rem);    /* hero */
  --tempo-mid:  clamp(1.5rem, 2.4vw, 2.2rem); /* section */
  --tempo-low:  17px;                         /* body */
}`}

When to break the rhythm

Once or twice a year. A campaign launch. A milestone. A guest art director. The rule is simple: the break is felt because the rule was kept. A brand system that is never broken is a system that's already broken — but a brand system that is broken every Tuesday is a system that doesn't exist.

Studio Nord designs the rule and the exception together. The exception is part of the document. We write it down so the founder can hand it to the next designer and the rhythm survives the hire.

studio interior with bookshelf and natural light
Studio Nord's commitment to an accessible, inclusive website — and how to give feedback.

We want everyone to be able to read our work and get in touch, regardless of how they browse. Accessibility is treated as part of the craft here, not an afterthought.

## Conformance

This site aims to meet **WCAG 2.1 Level AA**. We test with keyboard navigation, screen-reader spot checks, and automated audits as part of every release.

## What we've done

- **Semantic structure** — real headings, landmarks, and lists, so assistive technology can navigate the page.
- **Keyboard support** — every interactive element is reachable and operable without a mouse, with a visible focus state.
- **Colour contrast** — text meets AA contrast against its background in both the light and dark themes.
- **Reduced motion** — scroll reveals and transitions are disabled for visitors who prefer reduced motion.
- **Responsive text** — layouts reflow cleanly and stay usable when text is zoomed.

## Known limitations

No site is ever perfectly accessible. If you hit a barrier we've missed, we want to know about it.

## Feedback

Email [hello@studionord.dk](mailto:hello@studionord.dk) and we'll do our best to respond — and to fix the issue in a future update.
The typefaces, tools, and principles behind the Studio Nord website.

A short note on how this site is made — partly for the curious, partly because we believe the medium is part of the message.

## Typography

Headlines are set in **Fraunces**, a display serif whose optical-size axis is pushed to its display setting for the large editorial moments. Everything else — body copy, labels, and technical specs — is **Geist** and **Geist Mono**. All three are self-hosted, so the site renders correctly offline and makes no third-party font requests.

## Built with

- **Astro** — for the static, content-first architecture and View Transitions.
- **Tailwind CSS** — with a small, role-named token system, so changing a handful of variables re-skins the whole site.
- **Alpine.js** — for the few interactive touches (the menu, the theme toggle) that genuinely need state.

## Principles

- Performance is a feature: self-hosted fonts, responsive images, and no tracking.
- Light and dark are first-class, not an afterthought.
- The content lives in plain data and Markdown files, so the words can change without touching the layout.

## Credits

Photography is placeholder imagery, to be replaced with your own. Design and code by Studio Nord.
How Studio Nord collects, uses, and protects your personal data.

*This page is a starting template, not legal advice. Review it with a qualified professional before you publish — privacy obligations vary by jurisdiction. This draft assumes the EU's GDPR, since the studio is based in Denmark.*

## Who we are

Studio Nord ApS ("we", "us") operates this website. If you have any questions about this policy, reach us at [hello@studionord.dk](mailto:hello@studionord.dk).

## What we collect

- **Information you give us.** When you submit the contact form, we receive your name, email, and the details of your message. We use these only to reply to your enquiry.
- **Usage data.** Like most sites, our hosting provider records basic, anonymous request logs (IP address, browser, pages visited) to keep the service secure and reliable.

We do not sell your data, and we do not use it for advertising.

## Cookies

This site ships without tracking or marketing cookies. If you add analytics later, document it here and obtain consent where the law requires it.

## Third parties

We rely on a small number of processors to run the site — for example, our hosting provider and the service that delivers contact-form messages. Each only receives the data needed to perform its function.

## Your rights

Under the GDPR you can request access to, correction of, or deletion of your personal data, and you can object to certain processing. To exercise any of these rights, email us at the address above. You also have the right to lodge a complaint with your local data-protection authority.

## Changes

We may update this policy as the studio or the law evolves. The "last updated" date above always reflects the current version.
The terms that govern your use of the Studio Nord website.

*This page is a starting template, not legal advice. Adapt it to your business and have it reviewed before publishing.*

## Acceptance

By using this website you agree to these terms. If you do not agree, please do not use the site.

## Intellectual property

The design, text, and imagery on this site are the property of Studio Nord ApS unless otherwise credited, and are protected by copyright. You may view the content and link to it freely, but you may not reproduce, republish, or repurpose it without written permission.

## Acceptable use

Don't use the site in any way that is unlawful, disrupts its operation, or attempts to gain unauthorised access to our systems.

## Disclaimers

The site is provided "as is". While we work to keep it accurate and available, we make no warranty that it will be uninterrupted, error-free, or fit for a particular purpose.

## Limitation of liability

To the extent permitted by law, Studio Nord is not liable for any indirect or consequential loss arising from your use of the site.

## Governing law

These terms are governed by the laws of Denmark, and any disputes fall under the exclusive jurisdiction of the Danish courts.

## Contact

Questions about these terms? Email [hello@studionord.dk](mailto:hello@studionord.dk).

Unifying product and marketing to rebuild one of America's largest EV charging network websites

Impact at a glance.

  • 30% bounce rate reduction
  • 250K+ monthly visitors (up from 100-200K)
  • 2x average engagement time (1-2 min → 2-4 min)
  • 50+ pages redesigned in 12 months
  • WCAG 2.1 AA-compliant accessibility achieved
    •

    FIG. 02 —

    FIG. 03 —

    "The company needed someone who could bridge the gap between marketing ambitions and product reality."

    FIG. 04 —

    EVgo's website was broken.

    As one of America's largest public EV charging networks, they had a digital presence that didn't match their market position:

  • Fragmented experience: Marketing and product teams working in silos created inconsistent navigation, duplicate links, and conflicting styles.
  • Accessibility failures: Missing alt tags, poor color contrast, broken tabbing—basic ADA compliance was lacking.
  • Blocked marketing velocity: Every form, every landing page required engineering time. Hard-coded components meant weeks of delay for simple updates.
  • Poor performance: 3+ second load times and a 72% bounce rate.
    •

    The company needed someone who could bridge the gap between marketing ambitions and product reality.

    ↳The Challenge

    EVgo's website was broken.

import Placeholder from "../../components/Placeholder.astro"; import img01 from "../../assets/work/detail/kjeld/01.png"; import img02 from "../../assets/work/detail/kjeld/02.png"; import img03 from "../../assets/work/detail/kjeld/03.png"; import img04 from "../../assets/work/detail/kjeld/04.png"; import img05 from "../../assets/work/detail/kjeld/05.png"; import img06 from "../../assets/work/detail/kjeld/06.png";

Kjeld is a third-generation roastery on Vesterbrogade. We rebuilt their identity around the quiet ritual of the morning cup — a Fraunces wordmark, a single weight of Geist for the specs, and a packaging system that ages with the bag. The brief, in the founder's words, was simply: "Less."

FIG. 03 — Wordmark on letterpressed business card

FIG. 04 — Single-origin retail packaging

"The kerning was the conversation. Once we got the wordmark right, everything else became a footnote."

Astrid Lange, Founder

A system that ages with the bag.

We designed three packaging variants — house, single-origin, and reserve — that share a single grid and a single type system. The label is printed in one Pantone on uncoated kraft; the specs are stamped on at the roastery the morning the beans are bagged.

The marque itself is a custom drawing built on Fraunces, with the ampersand redrawn from a 1962 ledger we found at the roastery. We held the wordmark at 80% optical size on the bag — small enough that it reads as a quiet detail, not a logo applied.

The site followed the same logic. Built on a content-first static stack, with a single column of editorial type, generous line-length, and a shop drawer that stays out of the way until you ask for it. We shipped in ten weeks; the team has been editing it themselves ever since.
motion poster still in muted palette — typographic loop

import Placeholder from "../../components/Placeholder.astro"; import img01 from "../../assets/work/detail/meridian/01.png"; import img02 from "../../assets/work/detail/meridian/02.png"; import img03 from "../../assets/work/detail/meridian/03.png";

Meridian came to us in early 2025 with a brief that sounded simple — design us a flagship typeface, ship by autumn. What they did not say out loud, but became clear in the first week, was that they were also starting a foundry. The typeface would be the foundry's first public statement. We spent three weeks before any drawing on what that statement should be. The answer, in our notes from that month, was the word considered.

FIG. 02 — Meridian Sans Display Black, Q tail study

FIG. 03 — Variable axis weight transition, frame study

↳The system

The typeface as a publication.

Meridian wanted the typeface release to read like the first chapter of a publication, not the launch of a product. That re-framed every decision. The specimen book became less a marketing piece and more a small editorial object. The motion specimens became less a feature demo and more a series of typographic essays — short, looped, designed to be watched the way one re-reads a paragraph.

The variable font system gave us range across four axes — weight, width, slant, optical size — but range alone was not the point. The point was restraint. We worked with Meridian to define a small set of canonical instances within the variable space: a Display Italic for headlines, a Regular Text for body, a Mono companion for technical settings. Sixteen styles in total, each one defended.

The motion specimens followed the same logic. Twelve looped sequences, each exploring a single axis transition — never multiple at once, never decorative. A weight loop. A slant loop. An optical size loop. Meridian's foundry website now lives around these specimens, not around feature lists.

"We didn't want the typeface to launch.
We wanted it to arrive."

Magnus Holm, Designer

FIG. 04 — Specimen system, atelier wall, complete release set, 2025

↳Specifications

Meridian Sans · Release 1.0

{[ { k: "Typeface", v: "Meridian Sans" }, { k: "Family", v: "Display · Text · Mono companion" }, { k: "Styles", v: "16" }, { k: "Variable axes", v: "Weight (100–900) · Width (75–125) · Slant (0°–12°) · Optical size (8–96pt)" }, { k: "OpenType features", v: "liga · dlig · calt · kern · salt · ss01–ss06 · zero · frac · sups · sinf" }, { k: "Language support", v: "Latin Extended · Vietnamese · basic Cyrillic" }, { k: "License", v: "Desktop · Web · App · Variable" }, { k: "Released", v: "09 · Sep · 2025" }, ].map((row) => (
{row.k}
{row.v}
))}

A foundry, quietly.

Meridian Sans shipped in September 2025. The launch was deliberately small — a single email to the foundry's existing list, a quiet announcement on a Tuesday. No countdown, no waitlist, no influencer rollout.

In the four months since, the typeface has been licensed by 47 independent studios and 3 publications. Slanted Magazine selected it for their 2025 type recap. Brand New featured the launch as a case study in foundry restraint.

Meridian is now drawing their second release. We are not designing it. That, too, was part of the brief — to build something the foundry could carry forward on their own.

A Beach Day with Peggy: understanding Lupus written by Candy Cheng

import Placeholder from "../../components/Placeholder.astro"; import img01 from "../../assets/work/detail/peggy/02.jpg"; import img02 from "../../assets/work/detail/peggy/02.jpg"; import img03 from "../../assets/work/detail/peggy/02.jpg"; import img04 from "../../assets/work/detail/peggy/02.jpg"; import img05 from "../../assets/work/detail/peggy/02.jpg"; import img06 from "../../assets/work/detail/peggy/02.jpg";

She's Been Writing This Since She Was Eight, and Today, Peggythepanda.com is live.

The Story

When she was eight, she started writing a story — one she’d carry for years before the characters even had names. Eventually, that story became A Beach Day with Peggy: Understanding Lupus, about a girl named Peggy and her best friend, Spike. The girl who accepted her lupus without hesitation, and who helped make her childhood feel full and joyful despite everything.

FIG. 02 —

FIG. 03 —

"It’s about the kind of friendship that finds a way to include you, even when the world doesn’t quite know how."

FIG. 04 —

The Process

After more than 100 rejections from traditional publishers, Candy decided to self-publish. That’s when I got pulled fully in.

Designing for someone you love is its own discipline. And when the person you’re designing for is still living the story they’re trying to tell — still navigating a condition that hasn’t gone away — the work carries a different kind of weight. You have to hold their vision carefully. Your job isn’t to make it yours. It’s to make it more theirs.

The road from manuscript to finished book was longer and harder than either of us anticipated. Over three years, I stepped in at different points to help move things forward — developing the initial character designs, laying out scenes when the production process stalled, and eventually building everything you see at peggythepanda.com from scratch.

In the End

None of this was clean or linear. Most meaningful things aren’t. But the book is real, the site is live, and Candy’s story — the one she’s been carrying since she was eight — is finally out in the world.

If you know a kid living with a chronic illness, or one who wants to understand what their friend is going through, this book is for them. It’s for the Spikes of the world, too.

Go take a look: peggythepanda.com
